title: Fabricating fiber cavity mirror substrates compatible with high coupling efficiency
authors: Michael Caouette-Mansour, Thomas J. Clark, Valeria Mosso Tsedilkina, Jack C. Sankey
abstract: Fiber optical cavities offer small mode volumes and correspondingly strong light-matter interactions in an open Fabry-Perot geometry. However, existing fabrication techniques do not reliably produce substrates with surface profiles amenable to high mode matching between the cavity mode and fiber core, thereby limiting the achievable collection efficiency. Here we present a technique to fabricate fiber mirror substrates while using $\textit{in situ}$ reflectometry to constrain the achievable mode matching prior to coating. By measuring the back-reflection from freshly cleaved fiber tips, we pre-select 138 fibers compatible with 96.5-99.5% mode matching, and after a single CO$_2$ laser ablation pulse, these fibers remained compatible with 95.3-99.2\%. This simple technique provides rapid feedback during each stage of substrate fabrication, greatly enhancing the yield of viable fiber mirror substrates prior to (expensive) coating runs.
summary: This paper presents a novel, non-destructive technique using reflectometry to characterize fiber mirror substrates intended for optical cavities. By measuring back-reflection ($\eta_R$) during fabrication, researchers can accurately pre-select fibers that maintain high mode matching efficiency ($\eta_T > 95\%$). This significantly streamlines the experimental process by reducing reliance on costly post-fabrication characterization.
main_problem: The primary challenge is achieving high collection efficiency ($\eta_T$) in fiber optical cavities by minimizing losses caused by mode mismatch between the cavity mode and the fiber core at the mirror surface.
main_result: A simple, non-invasive technique using *in situ* reflectometry can pre-select viable fiber mirror substrates with high coupling efficiencies ($>95\%$), greatly improving experimental yield before expensive coating steps.
method: The authors developed a methodology combining cleaving and $	ext{CO}_2$ laser ablation on fiber tips, characterizing the resulting surface quality using continuous back-reflection measurements ($\eta_R$) via Superluminescent Diodes (SLEDs).
model_or_system: The system involves an open Fabry-Perot optical cavity formed by cleaved and ablated single-mode fiber ends. The theoretical analysis models the coupling efficiency based on surface imperfections like tilt angle ($	heta$) and radius of curvature ($ho$).
key_observables: Collection efficiency ($\eta_T$), measured back-reflection coefficient ($\eta_R$), surface parameters (tilt $	heta$, radius $ho$), and RMS roughness.
important_parameters: Target collection efficiency ($\eta_T > 0.95$); Wavelength of operation (1550 nm).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on approximations for small surface gradients, and the reflectometry measurement is assumed to provide a tight constraint on $\eta_T$, potentially bypassing the need for profilometry measurements of tilt angle.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the geometry relating coupling efficiency and reflection coefficients; one figure shows the experimental setup tracking $\eta_R$ evolution during ablation alongside AFM roughness scans; another plots measured $\eta_R$ versus theoretical parameters ($ho$, $	heta$) to constrain $\eta_T$.
paper_structure: The paper first establishes the physical limitation (mode mismatch) and then introduces the novel experimental technique: using reflectometry *in situ* during fabrication. Theoretical derivations are presented in appendices, culminating in a Bayesian analysis framework to calibrate the measured reflection ($\alpha$) against theoretical maximums ($\eta_{R,max}$).
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and open quantum systems because it directly addresses the engineering bottleneck—the coupling efficiency—required for building high-finesse cavities necessary for quantum memory or single-photon sources.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

FIG. 2. Experimental setup (a) Apparatus for ablating the fiber tip and estimating the achievable coupling efficiency. The reflectometry measurement uses light from a superluminescent light-emitting diode (SLED), which has a coherence length below ∼7 µm to eliminate standing-wave interference. The first photodiode (collecting power P1) monitors power fluctuations, and the second (power P2) measures light returning from the fiber tip. The end fiber of this reflectometer is spliced into a fiber spool from which the other end is mounted on a translation stage to move between ablation and profilometry. (b) Measured reflection ηR during ablation. The red fill indicates when the CO2 laser is on,...

FIG. 3. Measured Fibers and Expected Coupling (a) Measured (smaller) radius of curvature (ρ) and back reflection (ηR) on 138 fibers before (blue markers) and after (orange markers) ablation. The violet curve is the theoretical limit for a perfect core alignment (θ = 0). The black lines are contours of constant mode coupling (ηT ) with the indicated values (thin gray lines are half-values in between). The blue triangles indicate fibers with ρ ≥4000µm. (b) ηR as a function of the mode coupling (ηT ) for the same fibers with corresponding marginalized histograms on each axis. The solid violet line is the same limit line as in (a) with the marked values of ρ. The dashed purple line is the limit...
